denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter concerns the request your provider submitted for surveillance cardiac CT angiography of the thoracic aorta every 6 months to monitor an ascending aortic aneurysm. After review under the UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines, the request is denied as not medically necessary at the requested frequency. Determination date: May 19, 2026.

United's guidelines provide that surveillance imaging for a stable ascending aortic aneurysm of this size is appropriate at an annual interval. The requested 6-month cadence exceeds that interval, and the documentation does not support that imaging at this higher frequency is medically necessary.

Your request was received at 9:40 AM and this determination was issued at 9:44 AM the same day, following review by an Optum clinical reviewer.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific guideline and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, a peer-to-peer review with a United medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to file an internal appeal of this decision. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, along with any additional clinical information you would like considered. Appeals may be sent to the address shown on your Explanation of Benefits.

Sincerely,
UnitedHealthcare Medical Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 48-year-old man with a congenital bicuspid aortic valve and an associated aneurysm of the ascending thoracic aorta. Serial imaging shows the aneurysm has enlarged from 4.2 cm to 4.6 cm over the past twelve months (about 4 mm of growth), exceeding the rate at which closer surveillance is recommended. He has a first-degree family history of acute aortic dissection in his father at age 55, and his measured aortic size index is in the elevated range for his body surface area. His cardiologist requests cardiac CT angiography of the thoracic aorta every 6 months because documented interval growth and a strong family history of dissection are recognized indications for short-interval surveillance, which allows timely identification of the 5.0-5.5 cm threshold for surgical repair. The annual-only interval the insurer cites does not account for this patient's demonstrated growth and dissection risk. The request and the denial were timestamped only four minutes apart, which is inconsistent with any individualized review of the submitted aortic measurements and family history. The determination letter also provided no notice of the member's right to external independent review, a protection required for an adverse benefit determination.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches field-for-field (UHC, Medical Necessity, cardiology, 41-55, M, frequency_excessive). Bicuspid-valve aortopathy is cardiology; surveillance CTA realistic. Rationale enacts frequency_excessive (6-month exceeds annual guideline). Age 48 in band; not 71+, null employer_archetype fine.
evidence_quotes: "sub_tactic": "frequency_excessive"
the requested every-6-month surveillance cardiac CT angiography of the thoracic aorta exceeds plan guidelines

scenario_realism.reasoning: Frustratingly realistic. BAV aortopathy with documented interval growth and family history genuinely supports 6-month imaging per AHA/ACC; UHC insisting on annual is a slightly-misapplied guideline the context rebuts. The 4-minute turnaround and omitted external-review notice are real, frequently-litigated ERISA self-funded defects. Coherent dx/tx/demographics.
evidence_quotes: exceeds plan guidelines for a stable ascending aortic aneurysm of this size, asserting that annual surveillance imaging is appropriate
The request was received at 9:40 AM and denied at 9:44 AM the same day. The determination omits any notice of external review rights.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Hits all UHC fingerprints: 'UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines', brands as 'United', invokes an 'Optum clinical reviewer', uses 'documentation does not support'. No competing-insurer tells. Clear 5.
evidence_quotes: After review under the UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines
following review by an Optum clinical reviewer

denial_logic.reasoning: Frequency-based denial matching frequency_excessive: 6-month cadence exceeds the cited annual interval. Authentically shoddy — invokes 'a stable aneurysm of this size' without stating the diameter or a growth/risk threshold, and leans on circular 'documentation does not support'. Real guideline, not hallucinated; clear appeal room.
evidence_quotes: United's guidelines provide that surveillance imaging for a stable ascending aortic aneurysm of this size is appropriate at an annual interval.
The requested 6-month cadence exceeds that interval, and the documentation does not support that imaging at this higher frequency is medically necessary.

clinical_realism.reasoning: Credible: bicuspid valve with ascending aortopathy is a classic association; 4.2->4.6 cm/yr growth is plausible; the 5.0-5.5 cm surgical threshold matches guideline ranges for BAV aortopathy; family history of dissection and elevated aortic size index are recognized risk modifiers favoring short-interval imaging. CTA is the correct modality. No invented details.
evidence_quotes: congenital bicuspid aortic valve and an associated aneurysm of the ascending thoracic aorta
the 5.0-5.5 cm threshold for surgical repair

diagnosis_treatment_match.reasoning: Serial CTA surveillance is standard-of-care management for a thoracic aortic aneurysm below the surgical threshold (ACC/AHA aortic guidelines), especially with a bicuspid valve. Exact category match.
evidence_quotes: Bicuspid aortic valve with ascending aortic aneurysm, 4.6 cm (I71.2)
Surveillance cardiac CT angiography of the thoracic aorta every 6 months

diversity_delta.reasoning: Bicuspid-valve aortopathy with aortic surveillance CTA appears nowhere else; neighbours are syncope/loop recorder, stable angina/coronary CTA, RHC, ablations, PCSK9, etc. The shared frequency_excessive token (vasovagal/loop recorder) differs entirely in dx, procedure, and trajectory. Distinct point in feature-space.
evidence_quotes: dx=Bicuspid aortic valve with ascending aortic aneurysm, 4.6 cm (I71.2); tx=Surveillance cardiac CT angiography of the thoracic aorta every 6 months
dx=Recurrent vasovagal syncope (R55); tx=Implantable loop recorder

diversity_statistical.reasoning: Max trigram-Jaccard: corpus=0.311 (nearest=case_136_uhc_mednec), neighbours=0.0. Thresholds: warn>=0.35, fail>=0.5.

safety_redactor.reasoning: Aortic-aneurysm surveillance frequency appeal for a 48M. 'family history of dissection' is a relative's vascular event, not self-harm; preventive surveillance is the opposite of end-of-life. No suicide/self-harm, child abuse, IPV, or gender-affirming-minor content. The 4-minute-review frustration is not incitement. UHC commercial, in scope. No banned topic.
evidence_quotes: The patient is a 48-year-old man with a congenital bicuspid aortic valve and an associated aneurysm of the ascending thoracic aorta.
allows timely identification of the 5.0-5.5 cm threshold for surgical repair

The annual-vs-6-month dispute is an appealable disagreement, not a contradiction. No drug/dx/date/gender/site instability.
evidence_quotes: surveillance cardiac CT angiography of the thoracic aorta every 6 months to monitor an ascending aortic aneurysm
the aneurysm has enlarged from 4.2 cm to 4.6 cm over the past twelve months

overall_tone.reasoning: Cold procedural register: 'denied as not medically necessary at the requested frequency', bureaucratic vagueness ('of this size'), standard sign-off, no apology/warmth/marketing. Clinical context drifts mildly toward advocacy but stays factual. Letter near-perfect.
evidence_quotes: the request is denied as not medically necessary at the requested frequency

Sincerely,
UnitedHealthcare Medical Management

financial_auditor.reasoning: Entirely silent on cost: no dollar figures or out-of-pocket estimate. 'at no charge' refers to guideline copies. No Medicare/Medicaid conflation. Silent on cost liability — anchor 5.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific guideline and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision

Omits external-review notice — a realistic imperfection (the intended flaw), not penalized. Anchor 5.

The determination letter also provided no notice of the member's right to external independent review, a protection required for an adverse benefit determination.

demographic_validator.reasoning: Bicuspid aortic valve is the most common congenital cardiac anomaly, ~2-3x more common in males, with ascending aortic aneurysm a classic complication; age 48 is typical. Pronouns (he/his) consistent with M. No stereotyping.
evidence_quotes: The patient is a 48-year-old man with a congenital bicuspid aortic valve and an associated aneurysm of the ascending thoracic aorta.
He has a first-degree family history of acute aortic dissection in his father at age 55

scope_guard.reasoning: Insurer UHC (allowed); Medical Necessity (allowed); coverage self_funded employer-sponsored commercial (ERISA self-insured, in scope) with no Medicare/Medicaid/TRICARE/VA/marketplace framing; patient 48 (adult 18-89); only insurer-side internal appeal and peer-to-peer plus an external-review-notice gap, no live-filing/attorney-work/court-representation. All five PASS conditions met. (Re-judged with a fresh isolated subagent after an initial false-FAIL that wrongly treated self_funded as out-of-scope.)

"plan_funding_type": "self_funded"

date_sanity.reasoning: Determination May 19, 2026 in window; same-day timestamps ordered (9:40 received, 9:44 issued); 180-day appeal window standard; twelve-month growth and family history precede the determination plausibly. The 4-minute gap is a review-adequacy concern, not a date inconsistency.
evidence_quotes: Determination date: May 19, 2026.
Your request was received at 9:40 AM and this determination was issued at 9:44 AM the same day

citation_traceability.reasoning: Cites 'UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines'/'United's guidelines' with no module/edition/page and no fabricated alphanumeric code; defers the specific citation by offering a copy on request. Realistically vague — anchor 5.
evidence_quotes: After review under the UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines, the request is denied as not medically necessary at the requested frequency.
United's guidelines provide that surveillance imaging for a stable ascending aortic aneurysm of this size is appropriate at an annual interval.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Highly exploitable: no external-review notice (required procedural protection); a 4-minute turnaround inconsistent with individualized review; and an annual-interval guideline that assumes a 'stable' aneurysm while ignoring documented 4 mm/yr growth and first-degree family history of dissection. Only defense is the existence of an annual baseline guideline.
exploitable_weaknesses: Letter omits required notice of the right to external independent review for an adverse benefit determination
4-minute turnaround (9:40 AM to 9:44 AM) is inconsistent with individualized review of submitted measurements
Annual interval assumes a stable aneurysm but ignores documented 4 mm/year growth and first-degree family history of dissection
strong_defenses: United's guidelines do provide an annual surveillance interval for a stable aneurysm of this size, a legitimate baseline to cite
evidence_quotes: The determination letter also provided no notice of the member's right to external independent review, a protection required for an adverse benefit determination.
The request and the denial were timestamped only four minutes apart, which is inconsistent with any individualized review of the submitted aortic measurements and family history.
